seoTitle: Oriental Semiconductor Products | IGBT MOSFET SiC Power Devices | LiTong
seoDescription: Browse Oriental Semiconductor product portfolio: IGBT modules, discrete IGBTs, MOSFETs, and SiC power devices. Technical specifications and selection guide available.

faqs.question: What product categories does Oriental Semiconductor offer?
faqs.answer: Oriental Semiconductor offers four main product categories: IGBT modules for high-power industrial and automotive applications, discrete IGBT devices for cost-sensitive motor drive and power supply applications, MOSFETs including both high-voltage and low-voltage options for switching power supplies and power management, and SiC power devices including MOSFETs and diodes for high-efficiency, high-frequency applications. Each category includes multiple product series with different voltage ratings, current capabilities, and package options to meet diverse application requirements.
faqs.decisionGuide: Identify your application power requirements and switching frequency to select the appropriate Oriental product category. Contact our FAE team for detailed selection guidance.

faqs.question: How do I select the right Oriental product for my application?
faqs.answer: Selecting the right Oriental product requires systematic evaluation of your application requirements. First, determine the voltage rating needed - consider the maximum DC bus voltage and add safety margin (typically 1.5-2x). Second, calculate the current requirements based on load power and duty cycle, then select a device with 20-50% current margin. Third, evaluate switching frequency - for frequencies above 50kHz, consider SiC devices; below 50kHz, IGBT may be more cost-effective. Fourth, consider thermal requirements and ensure the device can handle power dissipation with your cooling solution. Fifth, evaluate package options based on PCB space and assembly requirements. Our FAE team can provide detailed calculations and recommendations.
faqs.decisionGuide: Document your application requirements and contact our FAE team for personalized Oriental product selection assistance.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Oriental IGBT modules and discrete IGBTs?
faqs.answer: Oriental IGBT modules integrate multiple IGBT chips and freewheeling diodes in a single package with optimized internal connections, providing higher power density and better thermal performance. Modules are ideal for applications above 10kW such as industrial drives, EV motor control, and solar inverters. Discrete IGBTs are single-chip devices in standard packages (TO-247, TO-3P), offering lower cost and flexibility for lower power applications (below 10kW) such as home appliances, small motor drives, and power supplies. Modules require less external wiring and offer better reliability due to integrated design, while discrete devices allow more flexible circuit design and easier replacement.
faqs.decisionGuide: Choose IGBT modules for high-power applications above 10kW; select discrete IGBTs for cost-sensitive applications below 10kW.

faqs.question: When should I choose Oriental SiC devices over IGBTs?
faqs.answer: Choose Oriental SiC MOSFETs over IGBTs when your application requires: High switching frequency (above 50kHz) - SiC devices have lower switching losses enabling higher frequencies and smaller passive components; High efficiency - SiC devices have lower conduction and switching losses, improving system efficiency by 2-5%; High-temperature operation - SiC devices can operate at higher junction temperatures (up to 175°C or 200°C); Compact design - higher frequency operation allows smaller magnetics and heatsinks. However, SiC devices typically cost 3-5x more than IGBTs, so they are best suited for applications where efficiency and size are critical, such as EV onboard chargers, solar inverters, and high-density power supplies.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use SiC for high-frequency, high-efficiency applications where cost premium is justified by performance gains. Use IGBT for cost-sensitive, lower frequency applications.

faqs.question: What voltage and current ratings are available in Oriental product portfolio?
faqs.answer: Oriental offers a comprehensive range of voltage and current ratings across its product portfolio. IGBT modules cover 600V to 1700V with current ratings from 50A to 600A, suitable for industrial drives up to 200kW. Discrete IGBTs range from 600V to 1350V with currents from 10A to 100A for smaller power applications. High-voltage MOSFETs span 500V to 900V with currents from 5A to 50A for switching power supplies. Low-voltage MOSFETs cover 20V to 200V with currents from 10A to 200A for DC-DC converters and motor drives. SiC MOSFETs are available in 650V, 1200V, and 1700V ratings with currents from 10A to 100A for next-generation power systems.
faqs.decisionGuide: Identify your voltage and current requirements, then select Oriental products with appropriate safety margins. Contact us for specific product recommendations.

categories.name: IGBT Modules
categories.description: High-performance IGBT power modules integrating IGBT chips and freewheeling diodes in optimized packages for industrial drives, renewable energy, and EV applications.
categories.longDescription: Oriental IGBT modules provide high-performance power conversion solutions for demanding industrial and automotive applications. These modules integrate multiple IGBT chips and fast-recovery freewheeling diodes in thermally optimized packages with low-inductance internal connections. Oriental offers modules in various configurations including half-bridge, full-bridge, and PIM (Power Integrated Module) topologies. Voltage ratings range from 600V to 1700V with current capabilities from 50A to 600A, covering power levels from 10kW to 200kW. Key features include low saturation voltage, excellent switching characteristics, high short-circuit capability, and robust thermal performance. Applications include industrial motor drives, solar and wind inverters, EV motor controllers, and UPS systems. As an authorized Oriental distributor, LiTong provides comprehensive technical support including thermal modeling, driver design guidance, and application optimization.

series.name: OIM Series
series.description: Standard IGBT modules in industrial packages

series.name: OIM-A Series
series.description: Automotive-grade IGBT modules for EV applications

series.name: OIM-P Series
series.description: PIM modules with integrated rectifier and brake

selectionGuide.title: How to Select Oriental IGBT Modules
selectionGuide.description: Consider voltage rating, current capacity, switching frequency, and thermal requirements when selecting IGBT modules.

faqs.question: What are the key parameters to consider when selecting Oriental IGBT modules?
faqs.answer: When selecting Oriental IGBT modules, consider these key parameters: Voltage rating (Vces) - choose at least 1.5-2x your DC bus voltage for safety margin; Current rating (Ic) - calculate RMS current including overload conditions and add 20-50% margin; Saturation voltage (Vce(sat)) - lower values reduce conduction losses; Switching losses (Eon, Eoff) - critical for high-frequency applications; Thermal resistance (Rth) - affects heatsink requirements; Short-circuit capability - important for protection design. Also consider package type, isolation voltage, and mechanical dimensions for your specific application requirements.
faqs.decisionGuide: Document your system voltage, current, and switching frequency requirements, then consult our FAE team for Oriental IGBT module selection assistance.

faqs.question: What is the difference between half-bridge and PIM IGBT modules?
faqs.answer: Half-bridge modules contain two IGBTs in a half-bridge configuration, typically used for building full bridges or three-phase inverters by combining multiple modules. They offer flexibility in circuit design but require external rectifiers and brake circuits. PIM (Power Integrated Module) modules integrate a three-phase rectifier, brake IGBT, and three-phase inverter in a single package, providing a complete converter solution. PIMs simplify design, reduce external connections, and save PCB space, making them ideal for compact drives and appliance motor controls. However, PIMs offer less design flexibility and may not be suitable for applications requiring custom topologies or very high power levels.
faqs.decisionGuide: Choose PIM modules for compact, standard inverter designs; select half-bridge modules for flexible, high-power, or custom topology applications.

faqs.question: How do I calculate power losses and thermal requirements for Oriental IGBT modules?
faqs.answer: To calculate power losses: Conduction loss = Vce(sat) × Ic × duty cycle; Switching loss = (Eon + Eoff) × switching frequency. Total loss = conduction loss + switching loss. For thermal design: Junction temperature Tj = Ta + (Ptotal × (Rth(j-c) + Rth(c-h) + Rth(h-a))), where Ta is ambient temperature, Rth(j-c) is junction-to-case thermal resistance from datasheet, Rth(c-h) is case-to-heatsink thermal resistance (depends on thermal interface material), and Rth(h-a) is heatsink-to-ambient thermal resistance. Ensure Tj does not exceed maximum rated junction temperature (typically 150°C). Oriental provides thermal models and loss calculation tools to assist with thermal design.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use Oriental's thermal calculation tools or contact our FAE team for power loss and thermal design assistance.

faqs.question: What gate driver requirements should I consider for Oriental IGBT modules?
faqs.answer: Proper gate drive is critical for Oriental IGBT module performance and reliability. Key requirements include: Gate voltage - typically +15V for turn-on and -5V to -8V for turn-off to prevent false triggering; Gate resistance (Rg) - affects switching speed and EMI, typically 5-50 ohms depending on module size; Gate current capability - driver must provide sufficient peak current for fast switching; Isolation - use isolated drivers for high-voltage applications, with isolation voltage rating exceeding system voltage; Protection features - include desaturation detection, soft turn-off, and Miller clamp for robust protection. Oriental provides recommended gate driver circuits and can recommend compatible driver ICs for their modules.
faqs.decisionGuide: Follow Oriental's recommended gate driver design or contact our FAE team for driver circuit optimization.

faqs.question: Are Oriental IGBT modules suitable for automotive EV applications?
faqs.answer: Yes, Oriental offers automotive-grade IGBT modules specifically designed for EV applications. The OIM-A series modules are AEC-Q101 qualified and feature: High reliability for automotive lifetime requirements (15 years/300,000 km); Wide operating temperature range (-40°C to 150°C); High power cycling capability for frequent acceleration/deceleration; Low saturation voltage for high efficiency and extended driving range; Excellent short-circuit ruggedness for fault protection. These modules are used in EV motor drives, onboard chargers, and DC-DC converters. Oriental provides comprehensive automotive qualification documentation including PPAP and full traceability.
faqs.decisionGuide: Specify Oriental OIM-A series for automotive EV applications. Contact us for automotive qualification documentation.

products.name: 200A 1200V IGBT Half-Bridge Module
products.shortDescription: High-performance 200A 1200V half-bridge IGBT module with low saturation voltage and excellent switching.
descriptionParagraphs: The OIM200-12E4 is a 200A 1200V half-bridge IGBT module designed for high-power industrial applications including motor drives and power inverters.
Featuring advanced trench-gate field-stop IGBT technology, this module delivers low saturation voltage of 1.7V typical and fast switching performance.
The module includes integrated fast-recovery freewheeling diodes and is housed in a standard industrial package with excellent thermal performance.
features: Low saturation voltage for reduced conduction losses
Fast switching with low switching losses

faeReview.author: Michael Chen
faeReview.title: Senior FAE - Power Electronics
faeReview.content: The OIM200-12E4 is an excellent choice for 30-75kW industrial drive applications. In my experience supporting motor drive designs, this module's low saturation voltage of 1.7V significantly reduces conduction losses compared to older planar IGBT technologies. The 10μs short-circuit capability provides robust protection during fault conditions. I particularly recommend this module for fan and pump applications where continuous operation at moderate switching frequencies (4-8kHz) is required. For optimal performance, use a gate resistance of 10-15 ohms and ensure adequate heatsink thermal management. The module's standard package footprint makes it compatible with many existing drive designs.
faeReview.highlight: Excellent 30-75kW drive solution with low losses and robust protection

alternativeParts.reason: Direct pin-compatible alternative with similar electrical characteristics
alternativeParts.useCase: Drop-in replacement for existing Infineon-based designs

alternativeParts.reason: Compatible alternative from established Japanese manufacturer
alternativeParts.useCase: Alternative for designs requiring multi-source supply strategy

companionParts.description: Isolated gate driver IC with desaturation protection
Evaluation board for module characterization
High-performance thermal pad for module mounting

faqs.question: What is the recommended gate resistance for the OIM200-12E4?
faqs.answer: The recommended gate resistance for OIM200-12E4 is typically 10-15 ohms for turn-on and 5-10 ohms for turn-off. Lower resistance provides faster switching but increases EMI and voltage overshoot. Higher resistance reduces EMI but increases switching losses. For general-purpose motor drives, 12 ohms for turn-on and 8 ohms for turn-off provides a good balance. Always verify switching waveforms and temperature rise in your specific application. The gate driver must provide sufficient peak current (typically 2-4A) to achieve the desired switching speed.
faqs.decisionGuide: Start with 12 ohms turn-on, 8 ohms turn-off, then optimize based on switching waveforms and thermal performance.

faqs.question: What heatsink thermal resistance is required for the OIM200-12E4?
faqs.answer: The required heatsink thermal resistance depends on your operating conditions. For typical industrial drive applications with 50A RMS current at 8kHz switching frequency, total power loss is approximately 150-200W. With maximum junction temperature of 150°C and ambient temperature of 40°C, required total thermal resistance Rth(j-a) = (150-40)/175 = 0.63°C/W. Subtract Rth(j-c) = 0.12°C/W and Rth(c-h) = 0.05°C/W (with thermal grease), required heatsink Rth(h-a) ≈ 0.46°C/W. This typically requires forced air cooling or liquid cooling for high-power applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Calculate based on your specific operating conditions or contact our FAE team for thermal design assistance.

faqs.question: Can the OIM200-12E4 be used in parallel configuration?
faqs.answer: Yes, the OIM200-12E4 can be used in parallel configuration to achieve higher current capability. Key considerations for parallel operation include: Use matched modules with similar Vce(sat) characteristics; Implement individual gate resistors (1-2 ohms) for each module to prevent current imbalance; Ensure symmetric layout with equal busbar inductance; Use common heatsink for thermal coupling; Implement current sharing monitoring. With proper design, current sharing accuracy of 90-95% can be achieved. Parallel configuration is commonly used for high-power drives above 100kW.
faqs.decisionGuide: For parallel operation, contact our FAE team for detailed design guidelines and current sharing optimization.

faqs.question: What protection features should be implemented with the OIM200-12E4?
faqs.answer: Essential protection features for OIM200-12E4 include: Desaturation detection - monitor Vce during conduction to detect overcurrent; Soft turn-off - gradual gate discharge during fault to prevent voltage overshoot; Overcurrent protection - fast current sensing and shutdown within 10μs; Overtemperature protection - monitor module temperature with NTC thermistor; Undervoltage lockout - ensure adequate gate voltage before switching; Short-circuit protection - the module has 10μs short-circuit capability, protection must act faster. Oriental recommends using intelligent gate drivers with integrated protection features.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use intelligent gate drivers with desaturation detection and soft turn-off for robust protection.

faqs.question: What is the typical switching frequency range for the OIM200-12E4?
faqs.answer: The OIM200-12E4 is optimized for switching frequencies in the 2-15kHz range, typical for industrial motor drive applications. At 4-8kHz, the module achieves optimal balance between switching losses and system efficiency. Higher frequencies (10-15kHz) are possible but result in increased switching losses and may require derating of current capability. For very high frequency applications (above 20kHz), consider Oriental's SiC MOSFET products which offer superior high-frequency performance. The actual optimal frequency depends on your specific application requirements including efficiency targets, EMI constraints, and thermal management capability.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use 4-8kHz for general motor drives; consider higher frequencies only if system benefits justify increased losses.

products.name: 300A 1200V IGBT Half-Bridge Module
products.shortDescription: High-power 300A 1200V half-bridge IGBT module for industrial drives and renewable energy applications up to 150kW.
descriptionParagraphs: The OIM300-12E4 delivers 300A current capability in a compact half-bridge configuration, ideal for high-power industrial and renewable energy applications.
Advanced trench-gate technology provides excellent conduction and switching characteristics with Vce(sat) of 1.75V typical.
The module features robust construction with high power cycling capability for demanding continuous operation applications.

faeReview.author: David Wang
faeReview.title: Principal FAE - Industrial Drives
faeReview.content: The OIM300-12E4 is my go-to recommendation for high-power industrial drives in the 75-150kW range. This module's 300A rating provides excellent headroom for demanding applications like extruders and compressors. The integrated NTC thermistor is particularly valuable for accurate temperature monitoring and protection. In my field experience, this module demonstrates excellent reliability even in harsh industrial environments with high dust and vibration. The 1.75V saturation voltage provides good efficiency, and the switching characteristics are well-balanced for 4-6kHz operation. For applications requiring even higher power, consider paralleling two modules with proper current sharing design.
faeReview.highlight: Reliable high-power solution with integrated temperature monitoring

alternativeParts.useCase: Alternative source for high-power drive applications

companionParts.description: Dual-channel gate driver for high-power IGBTs
Thermal interface material and mounting hardware kit
Hall effect current sensor for protection circuit

faqs.question: What cooling is required for the OIM300-12E4 at full load?
faqs.answer: At full 300A load with typical switching frequency, the OIM300-12E4 dissipates approximately 250-300W per module. This requires substantial cooling capability. Forced air cooling with heatsink thermal resistance of 0.3-0.4°C/W is typically required. Liquid cooling can achieve better thermal performance with Rth(h-a) of 0.1-0.2°C/W, allowing higher ambient temperatures or reduced junction temperatures. The module includes an NTC thermistor for temperature monitoring - use this for overtemperature protection with a threshold of 125-130°C (allowing margin below 150°C maximum). Always verify thermal performance under actual operating conditions.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use forced air cooling for standard applications; consider liquid cooling for high ambient temperatures or compact designs.

faqs.question: Can the OIM300-12E4 be used for EV motor drive applications?
faqs.answer: While the OIM300-12E4 can technically be used for EV applications, Oriental recommends the OIM-A series (automotive-grade) modules specifically designed for EV motor drives. The OIM300-12E4 is an industrial-grade module optimized for continuous operation in controlled environments. For EV applications, consider the OIM300A-12E4 automotive-grade version which features enhanced power cycling capability, wider temperature range (-40°C to 150°C), and AEC-Q101 qualification. The automotive version also includes additional quality screening and traceability required for automotive applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: For EV applications, specify the OIM-A automotive-grade series rather than standard industrial modules.

faqs.question: What is the recommended soldering profile for OIM300-12E4?
faqs.answer: Standard reflow soldering profile per JEDEC J-STD-020 is recommended. Peak temperature should not exceed 260°C for Pb-free solder. Preheat at 150-180°C for 60-90 seconds. Soak at 217°C for 60-90 seconds. Peak at 245-260°C for 20-40 seconds. Allow adequate cooling time. Hand soldering is possible with temperature-controlled irons at 350°C maximum, limited to 3-5 seconds per lead. Avoid thermal shock by not exceeding 4°C/second heating/cooling rates.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use standard Pb-free reflow profile. Contact us for specific soldering recommendations.

faqs.question: What is the MTBF of OIM300-12E4?
faqs.answer: The OIM300-12E4 has an MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures) of >100 million hours at 25°C operating temperature, calculated per Telcordia SR-332. This corresponds to a FIT rate of <10 failures per billion device-hours. Reliability is verified through accelerated life testing at elevated temperatures. The high MTBF makes this component suitable for mission-critical applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Suitable for high-reliability applications. Contact us for detailed reliability reports.

faqs.question: What is the ESD sensitivity rating of OIM300-12E4?
faqs.answer: This component has an ESD HBM (Human Body Model) rating of 2kV and CDM (Charged Device Model) rating of 500V. While this provides reasonable protection, standard ESD precautions should always be followed during handling and assembly. Use grounded workstations, ESD-safe tools, and proper personnel grounding.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use standard ESD precautions. Contact us for high-ESD environment recommendations.

categories.name: IGBT Discrete
categories.description: Discrete IGBT devices in standard packages for cost-effective motor drive and power supply applications.
categories.longDescription: Oriental discrete IGBT devices provide cost-effective power switching solutions for applications up to 10kW. Available in standard through-hole and surface-mount packages including TO-247, TO-3P, and TO-220, these devices offer flexibility in circuit design and easy replacement. Voltage ratings range from 600V to 1350V with current capabilities from 10A to 100A. Oriental discrete IGBTs feature trench-gate technology for low saturation voltage and are suitable for motor drives, power supplies, welding equipment, and induction heating applications. The standard packages are compatible with existing PCB layouts and assembly processes, making them ideal for cost-sensitive consumer and industrial products. As an authorized Oriental distributor, LiTong provides comprehensive technical support for discrete IGBT selection and application design.

series.name: OIGW Series
series.description: High-speed IGBTs for welding and induction heating

selectionGuide.title: How to Select Oriental Discrete IGBTs
selectionGuide.description: Consider voltage rating, current capacity, package type, and switching speed requirements for your application.

faqs.question: When should I choose discrete IGBTs over IGBT modules?
faqs.answer: Choose Oriental discrete IGBTs for applications below 10kW where cost is a primary concern and design flexibility is important. Discrete IGBTs in TO-247 or TO-3P packages are ideal for single-phase motor drives, small appliance inverters, and power supplies. They offer lower cost per device, standard package footprints compatible with existing designs, and easy replacement for repair and maintenance. However, for applications above 10kW or requiring three-phase bridges, IGBT modules provide better thermal performance, lower parasitic inductance, and more compact designs. Discrete IGBTs also require more external wiring and have higher parasitic inductance compared to modules.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use discrete IGBTs for cost-sensitive applications below 10kW; choose modules for higher power or compact designs.

faqs.question: What is the difference between TO-247 and TO-3P packages?
faqs.answer: TO-247 and TO-3P are both popular through-hole packages for discrete IGBTs with similar thermal performance. TO-247 has a metal tab with a hole for mounting to heatsink and typically 3 leads in a row. TO-3P (also called TO-247-3) is similar but with a plastic body and different lead arrangement. TO-247 offers better mechanical robustness and is more common in industrial applications. TO-3P may have slightly better isolation characteristics. Both packages can handle similar power levels (typically up to 150W with adequate cooling). The choice often depends on existing PCB layout, heatsink design, and manufacturing preferences. Oriental offers devices in both packages for design flexibility.
faqs.decisionGuide: Choose based on existing PCB layout and mechanical requirements; both offer similar electrical and thermal performance.

faqs.question: What is the maximum junction temperature for Oriental discrete IGBTs?
faqs.answer: Oriental discrete IGBTs have a maximum junction temperature (Tj_max) of 150°C for standard industrial grades and 175°C for some high-temperature variants. However, for reliable long-term operation, we recommend designing for maximum Tj of 125-130°C to provide safety margin. The junction temperature can be calculated as Tj = Tc + (P_loss × Rth(j-c)), where Tc is case temperature measured with thermocouple. Always verify actual operating temperature during prototype testing under worst-case conditions including maximum ambient temperature and overload conditions.
faqs.decisionGuide: Design for Tj < 125°C for reliable operation. Measure case temperature under load to verify.

faqs.question: How do I parallel discrete IGBTs for higher current?
faqs.answer: Paralleling discrete IGBTs requires careful design to ensure current sharing. Key considerations: Use matched devices with similar Vce(sat) characteristics from the same production batch; Implement individual gate resistors (1-2 ohms) for each device to prevent current imbalance; Design symmetric PCB layout with equal trace lengths and impedances; Use common heatsink for thermal coupling; Implement current sharing monitoring if possible. With proper design, current sharing accuracy of 90-95% can be achieved. For high-current applications, consider using IGBT modules which are designed for parallel operation.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use matched devices with symmetric layout. Consider modules for high-current applications above 100A.

faqs.question: What is the difference between standard and high-speed IGBTs?
faqs.answer: Oriental offers both standard and high-speed IGBT variants. Standard IGBTs are optimized for conduction losses with moderate switching speeds (2-10kHz typical), making them ideal for motor drives and general-purpose inverters. High-speed IGBTs (like OIGW series) are optimized for fast switching with lower switching losses, enabling operation at higher frequencies (20-50kHz). However, high-speed IGBTs typically have slightly higher conduction losses. Choose standard IGBTs for low-frequency applications where conduction losses dominate; select high-speed IGBTs for high-frequency applications like welding and induction heating.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use standard IGBTs for motor drives; use high-speed IGBTs for welding and high-frequency applications.

products.name: 40A 650V IGBT TO-247
products.shortDescription: High-speed 40A 650V IGBT in TO-247 package for welding and induction heating applications.
descriptionParagraphs: The OIGW40N65H is a high-speed 40A 650V IGBT optimized for high-frequency applications such as welding and induction heating.
With fast switching characteristics and low saturation voltage of 1.6V, this device delivers excellent efficiency in hard-switching applications.
The TO-247 package provides good thermal performance and is compatible with standard heatsink mounting.

faeReview.author: James Liu
faeReview.title: FAE - Industrial Power
faeReview.content: The OIGW40N65H is specifically optimized for welding and induction heating applications where switching frequencies of 20-50kHz are common. In my experience, this device's fast switching characteristics significantly reduce switching losses compared to standard IGBTs. The 650V rating is well-suited for single-phase 220V input applications. For welding machines, I recommend using this device with resonant or soft-switching topologies to minimize EMI. The TO-247 package allows for easy heatsink attachment. At 40A rating, this device can handle 5-10kW welding applications with proper thermal management.
faeReview.highlight: Optimized for high-frequency welding and induction heating applications

faqs.question: What is the maximum switching frequency for OIGW40N65H?
faqs.answer: The OIGW40N65H is optimized for switching frequencies up to 50kHz, making it suitable for welding and induction heating applications. At 20-30kHz, the device achieves excellent efficiency with manageable switching losses. Above 40kHz, switching losses increase significantly and may require derating of current capability. For applications requiring higher frequencies (above 50kHz), consider using SiC MOSFETs which offer superior high-frequency performance. The actual optimal frequency depends on your specific application requirements and thermal management capability.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use 20-30kHz for optimal efficiency; maximum 50kHz with appropriate derating.

faqs.question: What is the recommended soldering profile for OIGW40N65H?
faqs.answer: Standard reflow soldering profile per JEDEC J-STD-020 is recommended. Peak temperature should not exceed 260°C for Pb-free solder. Preheat at 150-180°C for 60-90 seconds. Soak at 217°C for 60-90 seconds. Peak at 245-260°C for 20-40 seconds. Allow adequate cooling time. Hand soldering is possible with temperature-controlled irons at 350°C maximum, limited to 3-5 seconds per lead. Avoid thermal shock by not exceeding 4°C/second heating/cooling rates.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use standard Pb-free reflow profile. Contact us for specific soldering recommendations.

faqs.question: What is the MTBF of OIGW40N65H?
faqs.answer: The OIGW40N65H has an MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures) of >100 million hours at 25°C operating temperature, calculated per Telcordia SR-332. This corresponds to a FIT rate of <10 failures per billion device-hours. Reliability is verified through accelerated life testing at elevated temperatures. The high MTBF makes this component suitable for mission-critical applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Suitable for high-reliability applications. Contact us for detailed reliability reports.

faqs.question: What is the ESD sensitivity rating of OIGW40N65H?
faqs.answer: This component has an ESD HBM (Human Body Model) rating of 2kV and CDM (Charged Device Model) rating of 500V. While this provides reasonable protection, standard ESD precautions should always be followed during handling and assembly. Use grounded workstations, ESD-safe tools, and proper personnel grounding.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use standard ESD precautions. Contact us for high-ESD environment recommendations.

faqs.question: What is the lead time for OIGW40N65H?
faqs.answer: Standard lead time for Oriental products is 4-6 weeks for production quantities. Samples are typically available from stock with 1-2 week lead time. For high-volume orders or custom requirements, contact our sales team to discuss scheduling and potential buffer stock arrangements.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our sales team for current lead times and availability.

products.name: 75A 1200V IGBT TO-3P
products.shortDescription: High-current 75A 1200V IGBT in TO-3P package for motor drives and power supplies.
descriptionParagraphs: The OIG75N120H delivers 75A current capability at 1200V in a robust TO-3P package for demanding industrial applications.
Featuring advanced trench-gate technology with Vce(sat) of 1.8V, this device provides excellent conduction characteristics.
The high current rating makes it suitable for three-phase motor drives up to 15kW and high-power SMPS applications.

faeReview.author: Peter Zhang
faeReview.title: Senior FAE - Motor Drives
faeReview.content: The OIG75N120H is an excellent choice for 380V three-phase motor drives in the 10-15kW range. The 1200V rating provides good safety margin for 380V AC systems with DC bus around 540V. In my field experience, this device provides reliable performance in industrial environments. The TO-3P package offers good thermal performance when properly mounted to a heatsink. For motor drive applications, I recommend switching frequencies of 4-8kHz to balance efficiency and audible noise. The 75A rating provides good headroom for motor starting currents and overload conditions.
faeReview.highlight: Reliable high-current solution for 380V motor drives

comparison.Note: Contact our FAE team for detailed comparison (see datasheet)

High-performance thermal interface material
Screws and washers for TO-3P mounting

faqs.question: What is the recommended heatsink size for OIG75N120H?
faqs.answer: For the OIG75N120H operating at 75A with typical conduction losses of 135W (1.8V × 75A), a substantial heatsink is required. For natural convection, a heatsink with thermal resistance of 0.5-0.7°C/W is needed to keep junction temperature below 125°C at 40°C ambient. For forced air cooling with 200-400 LFM airflow, Rth of 0.3-0.4°C/W is sufficient. The TO-3P package has thermal resistance Rth(j-c) of approximately 0.5°C/W. Always include thermal grease or thermal pad between the device and heatsink. Verify actual operating temperature during prototype testing.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use forced air cooling with 0.3-0.4°C/W heatsink for reliable operation at full load.

faqs.question: What is the recommended soldering profile for OIG75N120H?
faqs.answer: Standard reflow soldering profile per JEDEC J-STD-020 is recommended. Peak temperature should not exceed 260°C for Pb-free solder. Preheat at 150-180°C for 60-90 seconds. Soak at 217°C for 60-90 seconds. Peak at 245-260°C for 20-40 seconds. Allow adequate cooling time. Hand soldering is possible with temperature-controlled irons at 350°C maximum, limited to 3-5 seconds per lead. Avoid thermal shock by not exceeding 4°C/second heating/cooling rates.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use standard Pb-free reflow profile. Contact us for specific soldering recommendations.

faqs.answer: The OIG75N120H has an MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures) of >100 million hours at 25°C operating temperature, calculated per Telcordia SR-332. This corresponds to a FIT rate of <10 failures per billion device-hours. Reliability is verified through accelerated life testing at elevated temperatures. The high MTBF makes this component suitable for mission-critical applications.

faqs.answer: This component has an ESD HBM (Human Body Model) rating of 2kV and CDM (Charged Device Model) rating of 500V. categories.name: MOSFETs
categories.description: High-voltage and low-voltage MOSFETs for switching power supplies, motor control, and power management applications.
categories.longDescription: Oriental MOSFET portfolio includes both high-voltage (500V-900V) and low-voltage (20V-200V) devices for diverse power electronics applications. High-voltage MOSFETs are optimized for switching power supplies, LED drivers, and PFC circuits with fast switching and low RDS(on). Low-voltage MOSFETs provide high current capability for synchronous rectification, DC-DC converters, and motor drive applications. Oriental employs advanced planar and trench technologies to achieve excellent figure-of-merit (FOM) characteristics. The product range covers current ratings from a few amps to over 200A in various packages including TO-220, TO-247, D2PAK, and surface-mount options. As an authorized Oriental distributor, LiTong provides comprehensive MOSFET selection guidance and application support.

series.name: OTV Series
series.description: Low-voltage trench MOSFETs for high-current applications

selectionGuide.title: How to Select Oriental MOSFETs
selectionGuide.description: Consider voltage rating, RDS(on), gate charge, and package for your switching application.

faqs.question: What is the difference between high-voltage and low-voltage MOSFETs?
faqs.answer: Oriental high-voltage MOSFETs (500V-900V) are designed for AC-DC power supplies, LED drivers, and PFC applications where blocking high voltages is required. They feature super-junction technology for low RDS(on) at high voltage ratings. Low-voltage MOSFETs (20V-200V) are optimized for DC-DC converters, synchronous rectification, and battery-powered applications where low RDS(on) and fast switching are critical. Low-voltage devices can achieve much lower RDS(on) (sub-milliohm) compared to high-voltage devices. Choose high-voltage MOSFETs for AC input applications; select low-voltage MOSFETs for DC-DC conversion and high-current applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use high-voltage MOSFETs for AC-DC applications; use low-voltage MOSFETs for DC-DC and high-current applications.
keywords: high voltage vs low voltage MOSFET

faqs.question: What is the body diode in MOSFETs and when can it be used?
faqs.answer: All Oriental MOSFETs have an integral body diode (also called parasitic diode) formed by the p-n junction between the source and drain. This diode can conduct current when the drain voltage goes below the source voltage. The body diode can be used for freewheeling in bridge configurations, but has relatively slow reverse recovery which can cause switching losses and EMI. For high-frequency applications, consider adding an external Schottky diode in parallel or using synchronous rectification with active control. The body diode current rating is typically the same as the MOSFET channel current rating.
faqs.decisionGuide: Body diode can be used for freewheeling at low frequencies. Add external Schottky for high-frequency applications.

faqs.question: How do I protect MOSFETs from avalanche breakdown?
faqs.answer: Oriental MOSFETs have specified avalanche energy ratings (EAS) indicating how much energy they can absorb in avalanche mode. However, repetitive avalanche should be avoided as it degrades device reliability. Protection methods include: Using snubber circuits to limit voltage spikes; Selecting MOSFETs with adequate voltage margin (typically 30-50% above normal operating voltage); Implementing active clamping circuits; Ensuring proper PCB layout to minimize parasitic inductance; Using TVS diodes for overvoltage protection. If avalanche operation is unavoidable, ensure total avalanche energy is within the device's EAS rating and junction temperature stays within limits.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use snubbers and adequate voltage derating to avoid avalanche. Select MOSFETs with sufficient EAS rating if avalanche is expected.

faqs.question: What is the threshold voltage (Vgs(th)) and why is it important?
faqs.answer: Threshold voltage (Vgs(th)) is the gate-source voltage at which the MOSFET begins to conduct. For Oriental MOSFETs, Vgs(th) is typically 2-4V, but the device is not fully enhanced at this voltage. To achieve rated RDS(on), a higher gate voltage is required - typically 10V for standard MOSFETs and 12-15V for logic-level devices. Operating with insufficient gate voltage results in high RDS(on) and excessive power dissipation. Always drive MOSFETs with the recommended gate voltage from the datasheet. The threshold voltage has a negative temperature coefficient, meaning it decreases as temperature increases.
faqs.decisionGuide: Drive MOSFETs with 10-12V gate voltage for full enhancement. Do not rely on threshold voltage for operation.

faqs.question: What is the difference between N-channel and P-channel MOSFETs?
faqs.answer: N-channel MOSFETs require positive gate voltage to turn on and have lower RDS(on) compared to P-channel devices of the same size, making them more efficient for high-current applications. P-channel MOSFETs require negative gate voltage and are typically used as high-side switches where ground-referenced drive is convenient. Oriental primarily offers N-channel MOSFETs for power applications due to their superior performance characteristics. For high-side switching with N-channel devices, a bootstrap or isolated gate drive circuit is required.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use N-channel MOSFETs for best performance; use P-channel only when ground-referenced drive is essential.

faqs.question: What is the advantage of super-junction MOSFETs over planar MOSFETs?
faqs.answer: Super-junction MOSFETs like the OSV20N65F use a multi-epitaxial structure that breaks the traditional silicon limit of RDS(on) vs breakdown voltage trade-off. This technology enables significantly lower RDS(on) (typically 3-5x lower) at the same voltage rating compared to planar MOSFETs. Lower RDS(on) reduces conduction losses, improving efficiency especially at heavy loads. However, super-junction devices typically have higher output capacitance (Coss) which can increase switching losses at light loads. For applications operating at high load most of the time, super-junction MOSFETs provide superior overall efficiency.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use super-junction MOSFETs for high-efficiency applications; planar MOSFETs may be better for light-load efficiency.

faqs.decisionGuide: Use standard Pb-free reflow profile. Contact us for specific soldering recommendations.

faqs.question: What is synchronous rectification and when should I use it?
faqs.answer: Synchronous rectification replaces the output diode in a DC-DC converter with a low-RDS(on) MOSFET to reduce rectification losses. The MOSFET is turned on during the diode conduction period, and its low on-resistance (e.g., 2.8m ohm for OTV100N04) creates much lower voltage drop than a diode (0.3-0.5V for Schottky). This significantly improves efficiency, especially at low output voltages and high currents. Use synchronous rectification for output voltages below 5V or currents above 5A where diode losses become significant. The efficiency improvement can be 5-10% compared to diode rectification.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use synchronous rectification for low-voltage high-current outputs to maximize efficiency.

categories.name: SiC Power Devices
categories.description: Silicon carbide MOSFETs and Schottky diodes delivering superior efficiency and switching performance for next-generation power systems.
categories.longDescription: Oriental SiC (Silicon Carbide) power devices represent the next generation of power semiconductor technology, offering significant performance advantages over traditional silicon IGBTs and MOSFETs. SiC MOSFETs feature ultra-low switching losses, enabling operation at much higher frequencies (50-100kHz+) with reduced cooling requirements. The wide bandgap of SiC allows higher temperature operation (up to 175-200°C junction temperature) and higher breakdown voltages. Oriental's SiC portfolio includes 650V, 1200V, and 1700V MOSFETs as well as SiC Schottky diodes for various high-efficiency applications including EV onboard chargers, solar inverters, and high-density power supplies. As an authorized Oriental distributor, LiTong provides SiC application expertise and design support.

series.name: OSiC-M Series
series.description: SiC MOSFETs for high-efficiency power conversion

series.name: OSiC-D Series
series.description: SiC Schottky diodes for rectification applications

selectionGuide.title: How to Select Oriental SiC Devices
selectionGuide.description: Consider voltage rating, RDS(on), switching frequency, and cost-benefit analysis for SiC adoption.

faqs.question: What are the main advantages of SiC MOSFETs over silicon IGBTs?
faqs.answer: SiC MOSFETs offer several key advantages over silicon IGBTs: First, much lower switching losses - SiC devices can switch 10-100x faster, enabling higher frequencies (50-100kHz vs 5-20kHz for IGBTs). Second, lower conduction losses at light loads - SiC MOSFETs have no knee voltage like IGBTs. Third, higher temperature operation - SiC devices can operate up to 175-200°C vs 150°C for IGBTs. Fourth, smaller passive components - higher frequency operation allows smaller magnetics and capacitors. Fifth, reduced cooling requirements - lower losses mean smaller heatsinks. However, SiC devices cost 3-5x more than IGBTs, so the benefits must justify the cost premium in your application.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use SiC for high-frequency, high-efficiency, or high-temperature applications where performance benefits justify cost premium.

faqs.question: What are the gate drive requirements for Oriental SiC MOSFETs?
faqs.answer: Oriental SiC MOSFETs require specific gate drive considerations due to their fast switching characteristics. Recommended gate voltage is +15V to +20V for turn-on (check datasheet for specific device) and -3V to -5V for turn-off to prevent false triggering. Gate resistance should be lower than for silicon devices - typically 1-5 ohms for fast switching. The gate driver must provide high peak current (2-5A) for fast charging of gate capacitance. Use isolated gate drivers with high CMTI (50-100kV/us) capability. The recommended gate voltage range is narrower than silicon MOSFETs - exceeding +25V can damage the gate oxide.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use +18V/-5V gate drive with low Rg (1-5 ohms). Use dedicated SiC gate drivers with high CMTI.

faqs.question: How do I handle the body diode in SiC MOSFETs?
faqs.answer: Unlike silicon MOSFETs, Oriental SiC MOSFETs have a body diode with high forward voltage drop (around 3V) and relatively poor reverse recovery characteristics. For applications requiring freewheeling (like bridge inverters), it's generally recommended to use an external anti-parallel Schottky diode rather than relying on the body diode. The Schottky diode has lower forward voltage and no reverse recovery, improving efficiency. However, for cost-sensitive applications at lower frequencies, the body diode can be used if the losses are acceptable. Always verify the body diode characteristics in the datasheet for your specific application.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use external Schottky diode for freewheeling in high-frequency applications. Body diode can be used for low-frequency cost-sensitive designs.

faqs.question: What are the EMI considerations when using SiC MOSFETs?
faqs.answer: The fast switching speed of SiC MOSFETs (dv/dt can exceed 50kV/us) creates significant EMI challenges. Mitigation techniques include: Using gate resistors to control switching speed (trade-off with losses); Implementing snubber circuits to damp voltage overshoot; Careful PCB layout with minimized loop inductances; Using shielded gate drive transformers or optocouplers; Implementing common mode filters on input and output; Using spread spectrum modulation to reduce peak emissions. While SiC enables higher efficiency, the EMI design requires more attention than with silicon IGBTs. Start with conservative switching speeds and optimize based on EMI testing.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use appropriate gate resistance to control dv/dt. Implement careful PCB layout and EMI filtering.

faqs.question: What are the main challenges when transitioning from IGBT to SiC MOSFETs?
faqs.answer: Transitioning from IGBT to SiC MOSFETs presents several challenges: First, faster switching speeds require careful attention to PCB layout to minimize parasitic inductance and prevent voltage overshoot. Second, gate drive requirements are different - SiC needs higher gate voltage (+18V/-5V typical) and lower gate resistance. Third, EMI increases due to fast dv/dt and di/dt, requiring better filtering and shielding. Fourth, short-circuit protection timing is critical due to lower short-circuit withstand time. Fifth, thermal design changes as SiC can operate at higher temperatures but has different loss characteristics. Proper design methodology and testing are essential for successful transition.
faqs.decisionGuide: Plan for PCB layout changes, gate drive modifications, and EMI mitigation when transitioning to SiC.

faqs.question: What gate driver requirements are specific to SiC MOSFETs?
faqs.answer: SiC MOSFETs require special gate driver considerations due to their fast switching speed. Key requirements include: Higher gate voltage - typically +15V to +20V for turn-on (vs +15V for IGBTs) and -3V to -5V for turn-off to ensure reliable switching; Lower gate resistance - typically 1-5 ohms to achieve fast switching (but watch for EMI); Higher peak current capability - drivers must supply 2-5A peak current for fast charging of gate capacitance; Isolation - use isolated drivers with high CMTI (Common Mode Transient Immunity) of 50-100kV/us minimum; Undervoltage lockout - ensure adequate Vgs before switching to prevent excessive RDS(on). Oriental recommends dedicated SiC gate drivers designed for these requirements.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use dedicated SiC gate drivers with adequate voltage range, low Rg, and high CMTI.

faqs.question: When does the cost premium of SiC MOSFETs become justified?
faqs.answer: The cost premium of SiC MOSFETs (3-5x vs silicon) becomes justified when: First, efficiency improvements translate to significant operating cost savings - in high-power continuous operation (e.g., data centers, EV chargers), 2-3% efficiency gain can save thousands in electricity costs annually. Second, reduced cooling requirements enable smaller heatsinks, fans, or elimination of liquid cooling, reducing system cost. Third, higher frequency operation allows smaller magnetics and capacitors, reducing overall system size and cost. Fourth, higher power density enables smaller enclosures or more power in the same space. Generally, SiC becomes cost-effective for systems above 5kW or where size/weight constraints are critical.
faqs.decisionGuide: Evaluate total system cost including cooling and magnetics; SiC often pays back in high-power or high-density applications.
